text: 2-Methylbenzimidazole (MBI), chemical formula C8H8N2, molecular weight 132.16, is a single-component organic molecular crystal and a proton-transfer-type biaxial ferroelectric that remains ferroelectric above room temperature. It crystallizes in the non-centrosymmetric, polar monoclinic space group Pn (No. 7) with a pseudo-tetragonal metric, unit cell a = 13.9639 Å, b = 7.2107 Å, c = 13.9639 Å, Z = 8. MBI molecules lie flat in the a-b plane and form hydrogen-bonded chains, with two polar axes along the [110] and [1-10] directions and van der Waals contacts linking the chains. MBI single crystals have a remanent polarization of about 5.2 microC/cm2 and a low coercive field (~11 kV/cm). In this work, micron-thick MBI films were grown by low-temperature physical vapor deposition (~230 K) followed by restrained crystallization on warming; crystallization near 287 K produced ferroelectric films with remanent polarization up to about 2.5-2.6 microC/cm2 and an effective coercive field of about 87 kV/cm, with the [110] polar axis aligned out-of-plane. Ferroelectric switching was confirmed by polarization-electric-field hysteresis loops measured in situ. The crystal structure (CCDC 909441) was determined by single-crystal synchrotron X-ray diffraction at 300 K with an R factor of 5.11%, a density of 1.249 g/cm3, and a colorless plate habit. The film-fabrication study was reported by Yuan et al.
property_ref_title: Textured organic ferroelectric films from physical vapor deposition and amorphous-to-crystalline transition
